claim_text: Volcker disinflation shows that well-managed fiat with CB independence can restore price stability without a gold standard.
claim_notes: NK accepts long-run purchasing-power erosion but denies it implies gold-standard superiority. Phase 4C candidate: claim tests Volcker disinflation / CB independence restoring price stability; linked hypothesis tests post-1971 fiat purchasing-power erosion vs hard assets. Entity/outcome mismatch — relink to central_bank_independence_inflation_discipline or new volcker_disinflation_effect hypothesis.
hypothesis_claim: Over 50+ year horizons since the 1971 collapse of Bretton Woods, major fiat currencies (USD, GBP, EUR legacy components, JPY, AUD) have lost substantial purchasing power against hard assets (gold, broad commodity baskets, residential real estate). This is documented fact; the hypothesis re-establishes the pattern using provenanced public data.

covers_claims_entry.notes: NK claim that Volcker disinflation + CB independence restored price stability without gold standard; Phase 4C concern: entity/outcome mismatch — relink to central_bank_independence_inflation_discipline or new volcker_disinflation_effect hypothesis.

error_messages: treatment_tags: claim treatment_tags ['fed_independence', 'volcker_disinflation'] disjoint from hypothesis treatment_tags ['bretton_woods_collapse_1971', 'fiat_regime']

claim_text: Macron's 2017–2019 labour-market reforms (CDI/CDD flexibility, simplified dismissal procedures) produced measurable reductions in French unemployment duration without offsetting wage compression.
claim_notes: Phase 4C candidate: claim is France-specific Macron 2017-19 episode; linked hypothesis is generic cross-country EPL-vs-duration panel 1985-2023. Tangential — consider new france_macron_labour_reforms hypothesis or relink.
hypothesis_claim: Countries with stricter employment protection legislation — measured by the OECD EPL indicator (or comparable alternatives where OECD EPL is missing) — experience longer average unemployment duration, holding other controls constant. The hypothesis is consistent with the Mortensen-Pissarides search-and-matching framework: higher firing costs reduce both firing and hiring flows, lengthening the time unemployed workers spend searching for jobs. The effect on the unemployment LEVEL is theoretically ambiguous (lower inflows offsetting longer durations) and is NOT part of the pre-registered claim; only the duration effect is. Expected effect is sign-definite (positive correlation between EPL strictness and duration) with magnitude consistent with the Nickell 1997, Blanchard-Wolfers 2000, and OECD Employment Outlook (2020+) ranges.

covers_claims_entry.notes: Tangential coverage: France-specific Macron 2017-19 CDI/CDD flexibility episode partially addressed via FRA-in-sample, but specific ordonnances treatment not isolated. Phase 4C candidate: france_macron_labour_reforms.

claim_text: Trump's 2017 TCJA produced smaller investment and output responses than Laffer-curve advocates projected, consistent with New Keynesian estimates of corporate-tax-cut passthrough in a near-full-employment economy.
claim_notes: NK predicts positive but modest effects, not revenue-neutrality. Phase 4C candidate: claim tests Trump 2017 TCJA; linked hypothesis tests Reagan 1981-86 tax cuts. Era and entity mismatch — needs new tcja_2017_growth_effect hypothesis.
hypothesis_claim: Reagan's 1981–1986 marginal-tax-rate reductions produced measurable labour-supply response at the top of the distribution, with output growth exceeding the pre-reform trend.

covers_claims_entry.notes: Phase 4C concern: claim tests Trump 2017 TCJA while linked hypothesis tests Reagan 1981-86 tax cuts; era and entity mismatch requires new tcja_2017_growth_effect hypothesis.

error_messages: period: claim period [2017, 2021] disjoint from hypothesis period [1981, 1989]

claim_text: Schröder's Agenda 2010 reforms reduced German structural unemployment over the subsequent decade, vindicating targeted labour-market reform in a sticky-wage economy.
claim_notes: Phase 4D: original LLM reason appears hallucinated (it referenced Abenomics/ZLB/NK proposition, but the claim is about Schröder's Agenda 2010 in Germany and the hypothesis is the generic EPL-vs-duration panel). My read: tangential. Claim is Germany-specific Schröder Agenda 2010 episode; linked hypothesis is a generic cross-country EPL-vs-duration panel (1985-2023, 31 countries including DEU). Germany is in sample but not focal; the Agenda 2010 episode is a specific policy treatment not captured by OECD EPL indicator variation. Phase 4C candidate: relink to a germany_agenda_2010_structural_unemployment hypothesis or accept partial match via DEU-in-sample.
hypothesis_claim: Countries with stricter employment protection legislation — measured by the OECD EPL indicator (or comparable alternatives where OECD EPL is missing) — experience longer average unemployment duration, holding other controls constant. The hypothesis is consistent with the Mortensen-Pissarides search-and-matching framework: higher firing costs reduce both firing and hiring flows, lengthening the time unemployed workers spend searching for jobs. The effect on the unemployment LEVEL is theoretically ambiguous (lower inflows offsetting longer durations) and is NOT part of the pre-registered claim; only the duration effect is. Expected effect is sign-definite (positive correlation between EPL strictness and duration) with magnitude consistent with the Nickell 1997, Blanchard-Wolfers 2000, and OECD Employment Outlook (2020+) ranges.

covers_claims_entry.notes: Partial coverage: Germany-specific Schröder Agenda 2010/Hartz episode addressed only via DEU-in-sample panel variation; specific reform treatment not isolated. Phase 4C candidate: germany_agenda_2010_structural_unemployment.
